articles.content:
Catalyst Data · Getting Started

Governed entities, indicators, sources, evidence, questions, instruments, datasets, observations, quality, review, query, export, API, access, connectors, and refresh.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.11.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide provides a safe first run of Catalyst Data. It explains the product role, the main feature surface, the synthetic demonstration dataset, how to recognize a valid result, and when human or expert review is required.

Capability overview

  • Entities
  • Indicators
  • Sources
  • Evidence Ledger
  • Questions
  • Instruments
  • Datasets
  • Observations
  • Units and Methods
  • Quality Rules
  • Review and Revision
  • Persistent Workspaces
  • Saved Queries
  • Comparison Studio
  • Export Studio
  • Public API
  • Embeds
  • Institutional Access
  • Connectors
  • Refresh Operations

Prerequisites

  • A modern supported browser or runtime
  • The current installed product and service version
  • A new demonstration workspace
  • No confidential, personal, credential, or production information
  • A named reviewer for consequential output

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Entitiescreate the Community Center entitya stable entity IDsynthetic-demo
2Indicatorsdefine monthly electricity use with unit, method, and directiona reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
3Sourcesregister utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citationa provenance source recordsynthetic-demo
4Evidence Ledgerattach a bill and meter export to observationsan evidence chainsynthetic-demo
5Questionsstate whether use fell after weather normalizationa bounded analytical questionsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 20 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.
  2. Confirm the product and backend version before creating data.
  3. Create a synthetic workspace named CATALYST-DATA-RETROFIT-001.
  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Entities

Action: create the Community Center entity.

Expected result: a stable entity ID.

2. Indicators

Action: define monthly electricity use with unit, method, and direction.

Expected result: a reusable indicator definition.

3. Sources

Action: register utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citation.

Expected result: a provenance source record.

4. Evidence Ledger

Action: attach a bill and meter export to observations.

Expected result: an evidence chain.

5. Questions

Action: state whether use fell after weather normalization.

Expected result: a bounded analytical question.

6. Instruments

Action: define a meter-reading or import instrument.

Expected result: a versioned collection instrument.

Expected output

  • a stable entity ID
  • a reusable indicator definition
  • a provenance source record
  • an evidence chain
  • a bounded analytical question
  • a versioned collection instrument
  • A saved record that reopens without data loss
  • An export or handoff that identifies product, version, artifact, source, and review state

Verification

  • Every sample value remains attached to its identifier and classification.
  • Warnings remain visible and are not silently treated as approval.
  • The saved workspace reopens in a clean session.
  • The product and version shown in output match the installed release.
  • Another reviewer can understand how the output was produced.

Responsible use

Governed data is not automatically true or fit for every purpose; preserve source rights, privacy, definitions, uncertainty, and review.

articles.content:
Catalyst Data · How-to Guide

Governed entities, indicators, sources, evidence, questions, instruments, datasets, observations, quality, review, query, export, API, access, connectors, and refresh.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.11.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Catalyst Data, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Entitiescreate the Community Center entitya stable entity IDsynthetic-demo
2Indicatorsdefine monthly electricity use with unit, method, and directiona reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
3Sourcesregister utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citationa provenance source recordsynthetic-demo
4Evidence Ledgerattach a bill and meter export to observationsan evidence chainsynthetic-demo
5Questionsstate whether use fell after weather normalizationa bounded analytical questionsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 20 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Governed data is not automatically true or fit for every purpose; preserve source rights, privacy, definitions, uncertainty, and review.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

3Sourcesregister utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citationa provenance source recordsynthetic-demo
4Evidence Ledgerattach a bill and meter export to observationsan evidence chainsynthetic-demo
5Questionsstate whether use fell after weather normalizationa bounded analytical questionsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 20 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Entities: create the Community Center entity. Confirm a stable entity ID.
  2. 2. Indicators: define monthly electricity use with unit, method, and direction. Confirm a reusable indicator definition.
  3. 3. Sources: register utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citation. Confirm a provenance source record.
  4. 4. Evidence Ledger: attach a bill and meter export to observations. Confirm an evidence chain.
  5. 5. Questions: state whether use fell after weather normalization. Confirm a bounded analytical question.
  6. 6. Instruments: define a meter-reading or import instrument. Confirm a versioned collection instrument.
  7. 7. Datasets: create a monthly-energy dataset. Confirm a registered dataset.
  8. 8. Observations: enter January through March kWh with time, source, and status. Confirm traceable observations.
  9. 9. Units and Methods: define kWh and the normalization method. Confirm explicit unit and method records.
  10. 10. Quality Rules: flag negative, duplicate, missing, or out-of-range values. Confirm quality findings.
  11. 11. Review and Revision: correct a duplicate while preserving history. Confirm a revision record.
  12. 12. Persistent Workspaces: save datasets, views, queries, and notes. Confirm a reusable workspace.
  13. 13. Saved Queries: save a query for reviewed monthly observations. Confirm a versioned query.
  14. 14. Comparison Studio: compare baseline and retrofit months. Confirm a normalized comparison.
  15. 15. Export Studio: freeze the reviewed comparison into CSV, JSON, brief, and manifest. Confirm an integrity-protected export.
  16. 16. Public API: return approved energy observations. Confirm scoped structured data.
  17. 17. Embeds: embed an approved energy trend table. Confirm a controlled visualization.
  18. 18. Institutional Access: give reviewers access without publication rights. Confirm role-aware permissions.
  19. 19. Connectors: retrieve a public utility or indicator feed. Confirm a logged connector run.
  20. 20. Refresh Operations: refresh tariff data and reconcile exceptions. Confirm a refresh record and exception queue.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Catalyst Analytics R
  • Research Lab
  • Decision Studio
  • Site Intelligence
  • Knowledge Library
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • a stable entity ID
  • a reusable indicator definition
  • a provenance source record
  • an evidence chain
  • a bounded analytical question
  • a versioned collection instrument
  • a registered dataset
  • traceable observations
  • explicit unit and method records
  • quality findings
  • a revision record
  • a reusable workspace
  • a versioned query
  • a normalized comparison
  • an integrity-protected export
  • scoped structured data
  • a controlled visualization
  • role-aware permissions
  • a logged connector run
  • a refresh record and exception queue
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Governed data is not automatically true or fit for every purpose; preserve source rights, privacy, definitions, uncertainty, and review.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Catalyst Data: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Catalyst Data failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Catalyst Data · Troubleshooting

Governed entities, indicators, sources, evidence, questions, instruments, datasets, observations, quality, review, query, export, API, access, connectors, and refresh.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.11.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Catalyst Data. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Entitiescreate the Community Center entitya stable entity IDsynthetic-demo
2Indicatorsdefine monthly electricity use with unit, method, and directiona reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
3Sourcesregister utility data with publisher, URL, license, retrieval, and citationa provenance source recordsynthetic-demo
4Evidence Ledgerattach a bill and meter export to observationsan evidence chainsynthetic-demo
5Questionsstate whether use fell after weather normalizationa bounded analytical questionsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 20 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Observation rejectedrequired field, type, unit, or relationship is invalidcorrect the record or schema
Comparison misleadingdefinition, unit, period, or method differsuse a crosswalk or mark non-comparable
Query emptyfilters exclude all reviewed recordsinspect status, period, product, and workspace
Refresh failedauth, schema, or source availability changedpreserve prior snapshot and review exception

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Governed data is not automatically true or fit for every purpose; preserve source rights, privacy, definitions, uncertainty, and review.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Catalyst Data.

  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Functional surface

Review and Revisioncorrect a duplicate while preserving historya revision record
Persistent Workspacessave datasets, views, queries, and notesa reusable workspace
Saved Queriessave a query for reviewed monthly observationsa versioned query

Refresh Operationsrefresh tariff data and reconcile exceptionsa refresh record and exception queue

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Catalyst Analytics R
  • Research Lab
  • Decision Studio
  • Site Intelligence
  • Knowledge Library
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

Governed data is not automatically true or fit for every purpose; preserve source rights, privacy, definitions, uncertainty, and review.

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Catalyst Analytics R, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

4Indicator Registryregister annual energy savingsa reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
5Derived Indicatorscalculate baseline minus retrofit kWhderived values with method metadatasynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Statistical output requires method competence, source review, uncertainty communication, and domain interpretation; it is not automatic causal proof.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

articles.title: Catalyst Analytics R: End-to-End Sample Workflow

articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators

articles.content:
Catalyst Analytics R · How-to Guide

Reproducible R data intake, metadata, validation, indicators, comparison, uncertainty, sensitivity, stress, accounting, workspaces, snapshots, reports, and export.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.2.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This end-to-end workflow demonstrates every documented Catalyst Analytics R capability through one fictional resilient-community-retrofit case. The objective is not to prove a real-world conclusion; it is to verify the complete product workflow with controlled data.

Prerequisites

  • The current product release
  • A new synthetic workspace
  • The all-feature CSV and JSON
  • No production or private information
  • Time to review every checkpoint

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Data Intakeread the feature-example CSV or JSONa governed R data objectsynthetic-demo
2Source Metadataattach publisher, URL, license, citation, retrieval, field, and unit metadataa source-aware datasetsynthetic-demo
3Dataset Validationcheck required fields, types, missingness, units, and duplicatesa validation reportsynthetic-demo
4Indicator Registryregister annual energy savingsa reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
5Derived Indicatorscalculate baseline minus retrofit kWhderived values with method metadatasynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Data Intake: read the feature-example CSV or JSON. Confirm a governed R data object.
  2. 2. Source Metadata: attach publisher, URL, license, citation, retrieval, field, and unit metadata. Confirm a source-aware dataset.
  3. 3. Dataset Validation: check required fields, types, missingness, units, and duplicates. Confirm a validation report.
  4. 4. Indicator Registry: register annual energy savings. Confirm a reusable indicator definition.
  5. 5. Derived Indicators: calculate baseline minus retrofit kWh. Confirm derived values with method metadata.
  6. 6. Comparison: compare baseline and retrofit groups or periods. Confirm a comparison table and effect summary.
  7. 7. Uncertainty: apply an explicit ±8% measurement range. Confirm intervals and assumptions.
  8. 8. Sensitivity: vary tariff and occupancy inputs. Confirm a ranked sensitivity result.
  9. 9. Stress Testing: model 20% lower savings and 15% higher cost. Confirm stress-case outputs.
  10. 10. Accounting: reconcile monthly and annual energy totals. Confirm a balance check.
  11. 11. Workspaces: save data, indicators, assumptions, and outputs. Confirm a persistent analytical state.
  12. 12. Snapshots: freeze and restore complete workspace state. Confirm a fingerprinted snapshot.
  13. 13. Reproducible Reports: render methods, figures, results, and limitations. Confirm a rerunnable report.
  14. 14. Canonical JSON Export: export the reviewed comparison. Confirm a schema-versioned JSON record.
  15. 15. Browser Demo Parity: repeat the conceptual workflow in the public demo. Confirm a mapped result with parity limits.
  16. 16. Package Validation: run tests, examples, documentation checks, and R CMD check. Confirm a release-validation report.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Catalyst Data
  • Decision Studio
  • Research Lab
  • Workbench
  • Knowledge Library

Expected output

  • a governed R data object
  • a source-aware dataset
  • a validation report
  • a reusable indicator definition
  • derived values with method metadata
  • a comparison table and effect summary
  • intervals and assumptions
  • a ranked sensitivity result
  • stress-case outputs
  • a balance check
  • a persistent analytical state
  • a fingerprinted snapshot
  • a rerunnable report
  • a schema-versioned JSON record
  • a mapped result with parity limits
  • a release-validation report
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Statistical output requires method competence, source review, uncertainty communication, and domain interpretation; it is not automatic causal proof.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Catalyst Analytics R: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Catalyst Analytics R failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Catalyst Analytics R · Troubleshooting

Reproducible R data intake, metadata, validation, indicators, comparison, uncertainty, sensitivity, stress, accounting, workspaces, snapshots, reports, and export.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.2.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Catalyst Analytics R. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Data Intakeread the feature-example CSV or JSONa governed R data objectsynthetic-demo
2Source Metadataattach publisher, URL, license, citation, retrieval, field, and unit metadataa source-aware datasetsynthetic-demo
3Dataset Validationcheck required fields, types, missingness, units, and duplicatesa validation reportsynthetic-demo
4Indicator Registryregister annual energy savingsa reusable indicator definitionsynthetic-demo
5Derived Indicatorscalculate baseline minus retrofit kWhderived values with method metadatasynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Package not foundpackage is absent from the active libraryinstall reviewed source and confirm .libPaths()
Snapshot fingerprint mismatchvolatile or unordered state changedcanonicalize serialization and exclude runtime-only fields
Validation errormetadata, type, or unit is missingcomplete metadata or correct schema
Browser result differsdemo is conceptual rather than numerically identicaluse the R package for reproducible analysis

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Statistical output requires method competence, source review, uncertainty communication, and domain interpretation; it is not automatic causal proof.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

Core record fields

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility

Integrations

  • Workbench

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  2. Validate the all-feature sample.

Purpose

Capability overview

  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Model Workspace

2. Assumption Register

Action: record tariff, grant, discount-rate, and project-life assumptions with sources.

Expected result: a traceable assumption set.

6. Budget and Forecast

Action: compare actual, budget, forecast, and variance.

Expected output

  • a persistent finance workspace

Catalyst Finance · How-to Guide

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Catalyst Finance, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

This is not investment, accounting, tax, or legal advice; consequential financial decisions require qualified review and authoritative records.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

articles.content:
Catalyst Finance · How-to Guide

Governed financial workspaces, assumptions, drivers, statements, cash flow, budgets, scenarios, sensitivity, stress, valuation, capital, risk, review, export, and decisions.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.4.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This end-to-end workflow demonstrates every documented Catalyst Finance capability through one fictional resilient-community-retrofit case. The objective is not to prove a real-world conclusion; it is to verify the complete product workflow with controlled data.

Prerequisites

  • The current product release
  • A new synthetic workspace
  • The all-feature CSV and JSON
  • No production or private information
  • Time to review every checkpoint

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Model Workspacecreate CF-RETROFIT-001a persistent finance workspacesynthetic-demo
2Assumption Registerrecord tariff, grant, discount-rate, and project-life assumptions with sourcesa traceable assumption setsynthetic-demo
3Revenue and Cost Driversmodel square footage, kWh, tariff, maintenance, and implementation timingdriver-based projectionssynthetic-demo
4Three-Statement Modelconnect income statement, balance sheet, and cash flowintegrated statements and balance checkssynthetic-demo
5Cash Flowschedule project payments, financing, operating savings, and maintenanceperiodic cash flowssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Model Workspace: create CF-RETROFIT-001. Confirm a persistent finance workspace.
  2. 2. Assumption Register: record tariff, grant, discount-rate, and project-life assumptions with sources. Confirm a traceable assumption set.
  3. 3. Revenue and Cost Drivers: model square footage, kWh, tariff, maintenance, and implementation timing. Confirm driver-based projections.
  4. 4. Three-Statement Model: connect income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow. Confirm integrated statements and balance checks.
  5. 5. Cash Flow: schedule project payments, financing, operating savings, and maintenance. Confirm periodic cash flows.
  6. 6. Budget and Forecast: compare actual, budget, forecast, and variance. Confirm variance tables and explanations.
  7. 7. Scenario Management: create downside, base, and upside assumption sets. Confirm scenario-specific statements and metrics.
  8. 8. Sensitivity: vary tariff growth, savings realization, cost, and discount rate. Confirm a sensitivity table or chart.
  9. 9. Stress Testing: apply a 20% cost overrun and delayed grant. Confirm liquidity and recovery effects.
  10. 10. Valuation: calculate project NPV under the documented cash-flow timing. Confirm a valuation with explicit assumptions.
  11. 11. Capital Structure: combine grant, debt, equity, and repayment terms. Confirm a funding structure and obligations.
  12. 12. Risk Register: connect contractor, tariff, funding, and performance risks to mitigations. Confirm a finance risk record.
  13. 13. Evidence and Sources: link assumptions to tariffs, contracts, studies, and historicals. Confirm a source-linked model.
  14. 14. Review and Approval: record preparer, reviewer, exceptions, and approval state. Confirm a human-controlled review trail.
  15. 15. Exports: produce statements, scenarios, model JSON, and audit bundles. Confirm portable inspectable files.
  16. 16. Decision Studio Handoff: attach the reviewed scenario comparison to DS-RETROFIT-001. Confirm a typed handoff with model version.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Decision Studio
  • Catalyst Data
  • Workbench
  • Knowledge Library
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • a persistent finance workspace
  • a traceable assumption set
  • driver-based projections
  • integrated statements and balance checks
  • periodic cash flows
  • variance tables and explanations
  • scenario-specific statements and metrics
  • a sensitivity table or chart
  • liquidity and recovery effects
  • a valuation with explicit assumptions
  • a funding structure and obligations
  • a finance risk record
  • a source-linked model
  • a human-controlled review trail
  • portable inspectable files
  • a typed handoff with model version
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

This is not investment, accounting, tax, or legal advice; consequential financial decisions require qualified review and authoritative records.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Catalyst Finance: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Catalyst Finance failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Catalyst Finance · Troubleshooting

Governed financial workspaces, assumptions, drivers, statements, cash flow, budgets, scenarios, sensitivity, stress, valuation, capital, risk, review, export, and decisions.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.4.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Catalyst Finance. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Model Workspacecreate CF-RETROFIT-001a persistent finance workspacesynthetic-demo
2Assumption Registerrecord tariff, grant, discount-rate, and project-life assumptions with sourcesa traceable assumption setsynthetic-demo
3Revenue and Cost Driversmodel square footage, kWh, tariff, maintenance, and implementation timingdriver-based projectionssynthetic-demo
4Three-Statement Modelconnect income statement, balance sheet, and cash flowintegrated statements and balance checkssynthetic-demo
5Cash Flowschedule project payments, financing, operating savings, and maintenanceperiodic cash flowssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Statements do not balancetransaction, opening balance, sign, or link is incompletetrace the first failing period
Scenario identity mismatchworkspace or package reports the wrong scenario/versionrefresh identity metadata and validate
NPV seems implausibletiming, rate, unit, or terminal assumption is wronginspect dates and run a reference case
Export blockedreview or evidence requirements are incompleteresolve governance checklist

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

This is not investment, accounting, tax, or legal advice; consequential financial decisions require qualified review and authoritative records.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Catalyst Finance.

Prerequisites

  • A test environment
  • A backup and rollback plan
  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Decision Studio
  • Catalyst Data
  • Workbench
  • Knowledge Library
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

This is not investment, accounting, tax, or legal advice; consequential financial decisions require qualified review and authoritative records.

articles.content:
Catalyst Narrative Risk · Getting Started

Claims, sources, evidence, narrative mapping, risk dimensions, scoring, uncertainty, cases, comparison, review, approval, governance, and handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide provides a safe first run of Catalyst Narrative Risk. It explains the product role, the main feature surface, the synthetic demonstration dataset, how to recognize a valid result, and when human or expert review is required.

Capability overview

  • Case Workspace
  • Artifact Intake
  • Claim Decomposition
  • Narrative Mapping
  • Actor and Audience Context
  • Source Ledger
  • Evidence Ledger
  • Risk Dimensions
  • Scoring Method
  • Uncertainty
  • Counterevidence
  • Comparison
  • Review Workflow
  • Approval
  • Governance Log
  • Exports and Handoffs

Prerequisites

  • A modern supported browser or runtime
  • The current installed product and service version
  • A new demonstration workspace
  • No confidential, personal, credential, or production information
  • A named reviewer for consequential output

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk casesynthetic-demo
2Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifactsynthetic-demo
3Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim recordssynthetic-demo
4Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative mapsynthetic-demo
5Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.
  2. Confirm the product and backend version before creating data.
  3. Create a synthetic workspace named NARRATIVE-RISK-RETROFIT-001.
  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Case Workspace

Action: create NR-RETROFIT-001.

Expected result: a persistent narrative-risk case.

2. Artifact Intake

Action: register a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custody.

Expected result: a source-preserved communication artifact.

3. Claim Decomposition

Action: separate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claims.

Expected result: atomic claim records.

4. Narrative Mapping

Action: map frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied action.

Expected result: a structured narrative map.

5. Actor and Audience Context

Action: record speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power context.

Expected result: actor and audience records.

6. Source Ledger

Action: preserve origin, retrieval, version, and custody.

Expected result: a source ledger.

Expected output

  • a persistent narrative-risk case
  • a source-preserved communication artifact
  • atomic claim records
  • a structured narrative map
  • actor and audience records
  • a source ledger
  • A saved record that reopens without data loss
  • An export or handoff that identifies product, version, artifact, source, and review state

Verification

  • Every sample value remains attached to its identifier and classification.
  • Warnings remain visible and are not silently treated as approval.
  • The saved workspace reopens in a clean session.
  • The product and version shown in output match the installed release.
  • Another reviewer can understand how the output was produced.

Responsible use

Avoid automated accusation, profiling, or definitive judgments; preserve context, counterevidence, uncertainty, and human review.

articles.content:
Catalyst Narrative Risk · How-to Guide

Claims, sources, evidence, narrative mapping, risk dimensions, scoring, uncertainty, cases, comparison, review, approval, governance, and handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Catalyst Narrative Risk, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk casesynthetic-demo
2Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifactsynthetic-demo
3Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim recordssynthetic-demo
4Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative mapsynthetic-demo
5Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Avoid automated accusation, profiling, or definitive judgments; preserve context, counterevidence, uncertainty, and human review.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

articles.content:
Catalyst Narrative Risk · How-to Guide

Claims, sources, evidence, narrative mapping, risk dimensions, scoring, uncertainty, cases, comparison, review, approval, governance, and handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This end-to-end workflow demonstrates every documented Catalyst Narrative Risk capability through one fictional resilient-community-retrofit case. The objective is not to prove a real-world conclusion; it is to verify the complete product workflow with controlled data.

Prerequisites

  • The current product release
  • A new synthetic workspace
  • The all-feature CSV and JSON
  • No production or private information
  • Time to review every checkpoint

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk casesynthetic-demo
2Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifactsynthetic-demo
3Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim recordssynthetic-demo
4Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative mapsynthetic-demo
5Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Case Workspace: create NR-RETROFIT-001. Confirm a persistent narrative-risk case.
  2. 2. Artifact Intake: register a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custody. Confirm a source-preserved communication artifact.
  3. 3. Claim Decomposition: separate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claims. Confirm atomic claim records.
  4. 4. Narrative Mapping: map frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied action. Confirm a structured narrative map.
  5. 5. Actor and Audience Context: record speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power context. Confirm actor and audience records.
  6. 6. Source Ledger: preserve origin, retrieval, version, and custody. Confirm a source ledger.
  7. 7. Evidence Ledger: link each claim to supporting, challenging, and contextual evidence. Confirm claim-evidence relationships.
  8. 8. Risk Dimensions: assess accuracy, omission, uncertainty, harm, manipulation, governance, and reputation. Confirm dimension-level evidence and scores.
  9. 9. Scoring Method: apply the canonical transparent scoring rubric. Confirm a method-versioned score and rationale.
  10. 10. Uncertainty: record missing evidence, ambiguity, confidence, and reviewer disagreement. Confirm an uncertainty record.
  11. 11. Counterevidence: add contractor-capacity and timeline challenges. Confirm a balanced evidence view.
  12. 12. Comparison: compare draft and final announcements or two cases. Confirm a change and risk comparison.
  13. 13. Review Workflow: request factual, harm, and governance review. Confirm a review trail.
  14. 14. Approval: require authorized approval for an official assessment. Confirm an approval record and rationale.
  15. 15. Governance Log: record method changes, overrides, conflicts, and score revisions. Confirm an auditable event log.
  16. 16. Exports and Handoffs: send an approved risk summary to Decision Studio. Confirm a portable privacy-aware artifact.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Decision Studio
  • Knowledge Library
  • Product Support Platform
  • Site Intelligence
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • a persistent narrative-risk case
  • a source-preserved communication artifact
  • atomic claim records
  • a structured narrative map
  • actor and audience records
  • a source ledger
  • claim-evidence relationships
  • dimension-level evidence and scores
  • a method-versioned score and rationale
  • an uncertainty record
  • a balanced evidence view
  • a change and risk comparison
  • a review trail
  • an approval record and rationale
  • an auditable event log
  • a portable privacy-aware artifact
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Avoid automated accusation, profiling, or definitive judgments; preserve context, counterevidence, uncertainty, and human review.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Catalyst Narrative Risk. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk casesynthetic-demo
2Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifactsynthetic-demo
3Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim recordssynthetic-demo
4Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative mapsynthetic-demo
5Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Version identity mismatchpackaged contracts report inconsistent versionsregenerate identity files and run integrity tests
Score without evidencedimension has no cited rationaleadd evidence or leave unresolved
Overbroad conclusioncase scope does not support the generalizationnarrow the claim and state exclusions
Sensitive material exposedprivate or harmful content entered public workflowrestrict access and follow incident procedure

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Avoid automated accusation, profiling, or definitive judgments; preserve context, counterevidence, uncertainty, and human review.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

articles.content:
Catalyst Narrative Risk · Technical Reference

Claims, sources, evidence, narrative mapping, risk dimensions, scoring, uncertainty, cases, comparison, review, approval, governance, and handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Catalyst Narrative Risk.

Prerequisites

  • Current release notes and repository documentation
  • A test environment
  • A backup and rollback plan
  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk casesynthetic-demo
2Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifactsynthetic-demo
3Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim recordssynthetic-demo
4Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative mapsynthetic-demo
5Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Technical contract

Canonical identity

FieldValue
Product slugnarrative-risk
Product nameCatalyst Narrative Risk
Verified version1.5.0
Content pack1.0.0
Default article statedraft
Human reviewrequired

Functional surface

CapabilitySample operationExpected artifact
Case Workspacecreate NR-RETROFIT-001a persistent narrative-risk case
Artifact Intakeregister a public retrofit announcement with URL, date, version, and custodya source-preserved communication artifact
Claim Decompositionseparate cost, emissions, comfort, and timeline claimsatomic claim records
Narrative Mappingmap frames, causal stories, heroes, harms, omissions, and implied actiona structured narrative map
Actor and Audience Contextrecord speaker, institution, intended audience, affected groups, and power contextactor and audience records
Source Ledgerpreserve origin, retrieval, version, and custodya source ledger
Evidence Ledgerlink each claim to supporting, challenging, and contextual evidenceclaim-evidence relationships
Risk Dimensionsassess accuracy, omission, uncertainty, harm, manipulation, governance, and reputationdimension-level evidence and scores
Scoring Methodapply the canonical transparent scoring rubrica method-versioned score and rationale
Uncertaintyrecord missing evidence, ambiguity, confidence, and reviewer disagreementan uncertainty record
Counterevidenceadd contractor-capacity and timeline challengesa balanced evidence view
Comparisoncompare draft and final announcements or two casesa change and risk comparison
Review Workflowrequest factual, harm, and governance reviewa review trail
Approvalrequire authorized approval for an official assessmentan approval record and rationale
Governance Logrecord method changes, overrides, conflicts, and score revisionsan auditable event log
Exports and Handoffssend an approved risk summary to Decision Studioa portable privacy-aware artifact

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Decision Studio
  • Knowledge Library
  • Product Support Platform
  • Site Intelligence
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

Avoid automated accusation, profiling, or definitive judgments; preserve context, counterevidence, uncertainty, and human review.

Prerequisites

  • No confidential, personal, credential, or production information

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.

  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

2. Goal Definition
