articles.content:
Catalyst Grit · How-to Guide

Responsible voluntary goals, milestones, effort, context, support, rest, setbacks, strategy experiments, reflection, learning, progress, uncertainty, review, and export.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Catalyst Grit, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Goal Workspacecreate GRIT-LEARN-001a user-controlled goal workspacesynthetic-demo
2Goal Definitiondefine an eight-week goal to complete a reproducible energy analysisa bounded goal with completion criteriasynthetic-demo
3Milestonesbreak the goal into data, calculation, analysis, and briefing stepsa milestone plansynthetic-demo
4Effort Logrecord a 45-minute analysis session without moral judgmentan effort recordsynthetic-demo
5Context and Constraintsrecord limited evening availability, data access, and care obligationsa context recordsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Not for diagnosis, psychometrics, hiring, admissions, discipline, surveillance, ranking, or punitive evaluation.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

Verification: The result must preserve the sample feature ID, product version, lifecycle state, provenance, and any domain-specific unit or classification. Failure must return an actionable error without destroying prior state.

16. Decision and Research Handoffs

Purpose: Use Decision and Research Handoffs within the governed Catalyst Grit workflow.

Sample action: send a voluntary learning plan to Canvas.

Steps

  1. Open or create the synthetic retrofit workspace.
  2. Select or navigate to this feature.
  3. Enter the matching row from the CSV or JSON sample.
  4. Review required fields, sources, units, permissions, assumptions, and warnings.
  5. Run or save the feature operation.
  6. Compare the result with the expected result below.
  7. Add a review note and preserve the feature state.

Expected result: a scoped handoff receipt.

Verification: The result must preserve the sample feature ID, product version, lifecycle state, provenance, and any domain-specific unit or classification. Failure must return an actionable error without destroying prior state.

Expected output

  • a user-controlled goal workspace
  • a bounded goal with completion criteria

  • an effort record
  • a context record
  • a support plan
  • a sustainable effort plan
  • a contextual setback record
  • a small learning experiment
  • a qualitative learning entry
  • an advisory user-reviewable pattern
  • a multidimensional progress view
  • an uncertainty note
  • a human-controlled conclusion
  • a consent-aware bundle
  • a scoped handoff receipt

Whole-product verification

  • All 16 documented features are present or intentionally permission-restricted.
  • Every feature accepts its synthetic row or explains why it does not apply.
  • Errors preserve saved state and provide a recovery path.
  • Exports and handoffs retain provenance and version identity.
  • No private, confidential, or secret information appears in public output.

Responsible use

Not for diagnosis, psychometrics, hiring, admissions, discipline, surveillance, ranking, or punitive evaluation.

articles.content:
Catalyst Grit · How-to Guide

Responsible voluntary goals, milestones, effort, context, support, rest, setbacks, strategy experiments, reflection, learning, progress, uncertainty, review, and export.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This end-to-end workflow demonstrates every documented Catalyst Grit capability through one fictional resilient-community-retrofit case. The objective is not to prove a real-world conclusion; it is to verify the complete product workflow with controlled data.

Prerequisites

  • The current product release
  • A new synthetic workspace
  • The all-feature CSV and JSON
  • No production or private information
  • Time to review every checkpoint

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Goal Workspacecreate GRIT-LEARN-001a user-controlled goal workspacesynthetic-demo
2Goal Definitiondefine an eight-week goal to complete a reproducible energy analysisa bounded goal with completion criteriasynthetic-demo
3Milestonesbreak the goal into data, calculation, analysis, and briefing stepsa milestone plansynthetic-demo
4Effort Logrecord a 45-minute analysis session without moral judgmentan effort recordsynthetic-demo
5Context and Constraintsrecord limited evening availability, data access, and care obligationsa context recordsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Goal Workspace: create GRIT-LEARN-001. Confirm a user-controlled goal workspace.
  2. 2. Goal Definition: define an eight-week goal to complete a reproducible energy analysis. Confirm a bounded goal with completion criteria.
  3. 3. Milestones: break the goal into data, calculation, analysis, and briefing steps. Confirm a milestone plan.
  4. 4. Effort Log: record a 45-minute analysis session without moral judgment. Confirm an effort record.
  5. 5. Context and Constraints: record limited evening availability, data access, and care obligations. Confirm a context record.
  6. 6. Support Map: add a mentor, documentation, and quiet-workspace support. Confirm a support plan.
  7. 7. Recovery and Rest: schedule recovery and pacing. Confirm a sustainable effort plan.
  8. 8. Setback Records: record a missed milestone caused by delayed data access. Confirm a contextual setback record.
  9. 9. Strategy Experiments: compare 25-minute and 50-minute work sessions. Confirm a small learning experiment.
  10. 10. Reflection: record what helped, harmed, changed, or remains uncertain. Confirm a qualitative learning entry.
  11. 11. Learning Patterns: notice better progress with morning sessions. Confirm an advisory user-reviewable pattern.
  12. 12. Progress Measures: track milestones, confidence, wellbeing, support, and learning. Confirm a multidimensional progress view.
  13. 13. Uncertainty: mark low confidence when records are sparse. Confirm an uncertainty note.
  14. 14. Review: let the user reject or revise an inaccurate suggestion. Confirm a human-controlled conclusion.
  15. 15. Export: download only selected records. Confirm a consent-aware bundle.
  16. 16. Decision and Research Handoffs: send a voluntary learning plan to Canvas. Confirm a scoped handoff receipt.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Catalyst Canvas
  • Decision Studio
  • Research Lab
  • Product Support Platform
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • a user-controlled goal workspace
  • a bounded goal with completion criteria
  • a milestone plan
  • an effort record
  • a context record
  • a support plan
  • a sustainable effort plan
  • a contextual setback record
  • a small learning experiment
  • a qualitative learning entry
  • an advisory user-reviewable pattern
  • a multidimensional progress view
  • an uncertainty note
  • a human-controlled conclusion
  • a consent-aware bundle
  • a scoped handoff receipt
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Not for diagnosis, psychometrics, hiring, admissions, discipline, surveillance, ranking, or punitive evaluation.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Catalyst Grit: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Catalyst Grit failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Catalyst Grit · Troubleshooting

Responsible voluntary goals, milestones, effort, context, support, rest, setbacks, strategy experiments, reflection, learning, progress, uncertainty, review, and export.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Catalyst Grit. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Goal Workspacecreate GRIT-LEARN-001a user-controlled goal workspacesynthetic-demo
2Goal Definitiondefine an eight-week goal to complete a reproducible energy analysisa bounded goal with completion criteriasynthetic-demo
3Milestonesbreak the goal into data, calculation, analysis, and briefing stepsa milestone plansynthetic-demo
4Effort Logrecord a 45-minute analysis session without moral judgmentan effort recordsynthetic-demo
5Context and Constraintsrecord limited evening availability, data access, and care obligationsa context recordsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 16 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Progress score feels punitivecompletion or effort quantity dominatesinclude context, support, rest, learning, and user meaning
Missing weekno record was enteredleave missing; do not infer motivation
Private data visiblepermissions or export selection is wrongrestrict access and regenerate export
Pattern is inaccuraterecords do not support the suggestionreject or revise under user control

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Not for diagnosis, psychometrics, hiring, admissions, discipline, surveillance, ranking, or punitive evaluation.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Catalyst Grit.

Prerequisites

  • Current release notes and repository documentation
  • A test environment
  • A backup and rollback plan
  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Sample data

Technical contract

Canonical identity

FieldValue
Product slugcatalyst-grit
Product nameCatalyst Grit

Human reviewrequired

Functional surface

CapabilitySample operationExpected artifact
Goal Workspacecreate GRIT-LEARN-001a user-controlled goal workspace
Goal Definitiondefine an eight-week goal to complete a reproducible energy analysisa bounded goal with completion criteria
Milestonesbreak the goal into data, calculation, analysis, and briefing stepsa milestone plan

Context and Constraintsrecord limited evening availability, data access, and care obligationsa context record

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Catalyst Canvas
  • Decision Studio
  • Research Lab
  • Product Support Platform
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

Not for diagnosis, psychometrics, hiring, admissions, discipline, surveillance, ranking, or punitive evaluation.

articles.content:
Global Impact Catalyst · Getting Started

Impact strategy, participation, theory of change, activities, outcomes, indicators, evidence, contribution, equity, safeguards, learning, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and mapping.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide provides a safe first run of Global Impact Catalyst. It explains the product role, the main feature surface, the synthetic demonstration dataset, how to recognize a valid result, and when human or expert review is required.

Capability overview

  • Impact Workspace
  • Problem and Context
  • Stakeholder Participation
  • Theory of Change
  • Activities and Outputs
  • Outcomes
  • Indicators
  • Baselines and Targets
  • Evidence
  • Contribution and Attribution
  • Equity and Distribution
  • Risk and Safeguards
  • Learning Agenda
  • Monitoring
  • Evaluation
  • Impact Reporting
  • SDG and Framework Mapping
  • Decision Handoffs

Prerequisites

  • A modern supported browser or runtime
  • The current installed product and service version
  • A new demonstration workspace
  • No confidential, personal, credential, or production information
  • A named reviewer for consequential output

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Impact Workspacecreate GIC-RETROFIT-001a governed program-impact workspacesynthetic-demo
2Problem and Contextdescribe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraintsa contextual problem statementsynthetic-demo
3Stakeholder Participationrecord staff and community participation in framing and reviewparticipation and consent recordssynthetic-demo
4Theory of Changeconnect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptionsa reviewable causal pathwaysynthetic-demo
5Activities and Outputsrecord control installation, envelope work, and staff trainingactivity and output recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 18 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.
  2. Confirm the product and backend version before creating data.
  3. Create a synthetic workspace named GLOBAL-IMPACT-CATALYST-RETROFIT-001.
  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Impact Workspace

Action: create GIC-RETROFIT-001.

Expected result: a governed program-impact workspace.

2. Problem and Context

Action: describe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraints.

Expected result: a contextual problem statement.

3. Stakeholder Participation

Action: record staff and community participation in framing and review.

Expected result: participation and consent records.

4. Theory of Change

Action: connect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions.

Expected result: a reviewable causal pathway.

5. Activities and Outputs

Action: record control installation, envelope work, and staff training.

Expected result: activity and output records.

6. Outcomes

Action: define short-, medium-, and long-term energy and comfort changes.

Expected result: outcome definitions.

Expected output

  • a governed program-impact workspace
  • a contextual problem statement
  • participation and consent records
  • a reviewable causal pathway
  • activity and output records
  • outcome definitions
  • A saved record that reopens without data loss
  • An export or handoff that identifies product, version, artifact, source, and review state

Verification

  • Every sample value remains attached to its identifier and classification.
  • Warnings remain visible and are not silently treated as approval.
  • The saved workspace reopens in a clean session.
  • The product and version shown in output match the installed release.
  • Another reviewer can understand how the output was produced.

Responsible use

Avoid impact washing, greenwashing, causal overclaim, token participation, and hidden distributional harm.

Global Impact Catalyst · How-to Guide

Impact strategy, participation, theory of change, activities, outcomes, indicators, evidence, contribution, equity, safeguards, learning, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and mapping.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Global Impact Catalyst, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Impact Workspacecreate GIC-RETROFIT-001a governed program-impact workspacesynthetic-demo
2Problem and Contextdescribe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraintsa contextual problem statementsynthetic-demo
3Stakeholder Participationrecord staff and community participation in framing and reviewparticipation and consent recordssynthetic-demo
4Theory of Changeconnect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptionsa reviewable causal pathwaysynthetic-demo
5Activities and Outputsrecord control installation, envelope work, and staff trainingactivity and output recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 18 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Avoid impact washing, greenwashing, causal overclaim, token participation, and hidden distributional harm.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

articles.title: Global Impact Catalyst: End-to-End Sample Workflow
articles.summary: Complete an all-feature retrofit demonstration in Global Impact Catalyst.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Global Impact Catalyst · How-to Guide

Impact strategy, participation, theory of change, activities, outcomes, indicators, evidence, contribution, equity, safeguards, learning, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and mapping.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This end-to-end workflow demonstrates every documented Global Impact Catalyst capability through one fictional resilient-community-retrofit case. The objective is not to prove a real-world conclusion; it is to verify the complete product workflow with controlled data.

Prerequisites

  • The current product release
  • A new synthetic workspace
  • The all-feature CSV and JSON
  • No production or private information
  • Time to review every checkpoint

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Impact Workspacecreate GIC-RETROFIT-001a governed program-impact workspacesynthetic-demo
2Problem and Contextdescribe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraintsa contextual problem statementsynthetic-demo
3Stakeholder Participationrecord staff and community participation in framing and reviewparticipation and consent recordssynthetic-demo
4Theory of Changeconnect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptionsa reviewable causal pathwaysynthetic-demo
5Activities and Outputsrecord control installation, envelope work, and staff trainingactivity and output recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 18 feature rows.

Procedure

Scenario

A community center is assessing energy, comfort, financing, implementation, evidence, and monitoring needs. Use only the supplied fictional records.

  1. 1. Impact Workspace: create GIC-RETROFIT-001. Confirm a governed program-impact workspace.
  2. 2. Problem and Context: describe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraints. Confirm a contextual problem statement.
  3. 3. Stakeholder Participation: record staff and community participation in framing and review. Confirm participation and consent records.
  4. 4. Theory of Change: connect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions. Confirm a reviewable causal pathway.
  5. 5. Activities and Outputs: record control installation, envelope work, and staff training. Confirm activity and output records.
  6. 6. Outcomes: define short-, medium-, and long-term energy and comfort changes. Confirm outcome definitions.
  7. 7. Indicators: specify kWh, complaints, training, method, frequency, owner, and disaggregation. Confirm indicator records.
  8. 8. Baselines and Targets: record baseline 45,000 kWh/month and a justified 36,000 target. Confirm baseline and target records.
  9. 9. Evidence: link meter data, surveys, documents, and evaluation findings. Confirm evidence tied to outcomes.
  10. 10. Contribution and Attribution: record weather, occupancy, and other influences. Confirm a contribution narrative with limits.
  11. 11. Equity and Distribution: disaggregate comfort by room use and accessibility need. Confirm distributional findings.
  12. 12. Risk and Safeguards: record disruption, exclusion, data, implementation, and reputation risks. Confirm risk and safeguard records.
  13. 13. Learning Agenda: ask whether controls remain usable after six months. Confirm a prioritized learning plan.
  14. 14. Monitoring: import routine monthly energy and comfort observations. Confirm a monitoring series.
  15. 15. Evaluation: document an appropriate before/after or interrupted-time-series method. Confirm an evaluation plan and findings.
  16. 16. Impact Reporting: publish a six-month brief with evidence and limits. Confirm a reviewable impact report.
  17. 17. SDG and Framework Mapping: map relevant work to SDG 7 and 11 without implying certification. Confirm a contextual framework mapping.
  18. 18. Decision Handoffs: attach approved outcome and risk records to Decision Studio. Confirm a typed impact handoff.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Catalyst Data
  • Decision Studio
  • Knowledge Library
  • Site Intelligence
  • Catalyst Finance
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • a governed program-impact workspace
  • a contextual problem statement
  • participation and consent records
  • a reviewable causal pathway
  • activity and output records
  • outcome definitions
  • indicator records
  • baseline and target records
  • evidence tied to outcomes
  • a contribution narrative with limits
  • distributional findings
  • risk and safeguard records
  • a prioritized learning plan
  • a monitoring series
  • an evaluation plan and findings
  • a reviewable impact report
  • a contextual framework mapping
  • a typed impact handoff
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Avoid impact washing, greenwashing, causal overclaim, token participation, and hidden distributional harm.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Global Impact Catalyst: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Global Impact Catalyst failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.content:
Global Impact Catalyst · Troubleshooting

Impact strategy, participation, theory of change, activities, outcomes, indicators, evidence, contribution, equity, safeguards, learning, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and mapping.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Global Impact Catalyst. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Impact Workspacecreate GIC-RETROFIT-001a governed program-impact workspacesynthetic-demo
2Problem and Contextdescribe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraintsa contextual problem statementsynthetic-demo
3Stakeholder Participationrecord staff and community participation in framing and reviewparticipation and consent recordssynthetic-demo
4Theory of Changeconnect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptionsa reviewable causal pathwaysynthetic-demo
5Activities and Outputsrecord control installation, envelope work, and staff trainingactivity and output recordssynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 18 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Impact claim unsupportedoutcome lacks evidence or causal claim is too strongnarrow claim and state contribution limits
Target has no rationaletarget lacks baseline, benchmark, or decision basisdocument basis and uncertainty
Stakeholder gapaffected groups are absentadd participation or state limitation
Framework mapping overclaimsmapping is presented as certification or endorsementuse descriptive boundary language

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Avoid impact washing, greenwashing, causal overclaim, token participation, and hidden distributional harm.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

Global Impact Catalyst · Technical Reference

Impact strategy, participation, theory of change, activities, outcomes, indicators, evidence, contribution, equity, safeguards, learning, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and mapping.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 1.5.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Global Impact Catalyst.

Prerequisites

  • Current release notes and repository documentation
  • A test environment
  • A backup and rollback plan
  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

5Activities and Outputsrecord control installation, envelope work, and staff trainingactivity and output recordssynthetic-demo

Technical contract

Canonical identity

FieldValue
Product slugglobal-impact-catalyst
Product nameGlobal Impact Catalyst
Verified version1.5.0
Content pack1.0.0
Default article statedraft
Human reviewrequired

Functional surface

CapabilitySample operationExpected artifact
Impact Workspacecreate GIC-RETROFIT-001a governed program-impact workspace
Problem and Contextdescribe energy burden, comfort inequity, affected groups, causes, and constraintsa contextual problem statement
Stakeholder Participationrecord staff and community participation in framing and reviewparticipation and consent records
Theory of Changeconnect inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and assumptionsa reviewable causal pathway

Evidencelink meter data, surveys, documents, and evaluation findingsevidence tied to outcomes
Contribution and Attributionrecord weather, occupancy, and other influencesa contribution narrative with limits
Equity and Distributiondisaggregate comfort by room use and accessibility needdistributional findings

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Catalyst Data
  • Decision Studio
  • Knowledge Library
  • Site Intelligence
  • Catalyst Finance
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

Avoid impact washing, greenwashing, causal overclaim, token participation, and hidden distributional harm.

articles.content:
Product Support and Feedback Platform · Getting Started

Public product documentation, guided resolution, known issues, releases, ideas, voting, surveys, feedback, gaps, onboarding, import, governance, repository sync, reliability, incidents, journeys, operations, and private handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 5.1.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide provides a safe first run of Product Support and Feedback Platform. It explains the product role, the main feature surface, the synthetic demonstration dataset, how to recognize a valid result, and when human or expert review is required.

Capability overview

  • Product Taxonomy
  • Support Articles
  • Documentation Collections
  • Article Types
  • Guided Resolution
  • Known Issues
  • Release Intelligence
  • Public Ideas
  • Voting
  • Forms and Surveys
  • Article Feedback
  • Failed Search Intelligence
  • Documentation Gaps
  • Product Onboarding
  • Content Import and Export
  • Editorial Governance
  • Repository Sync
  • Reliability Analytics
  • Platform Incidents
  • Cross-Product Journeys
  • Connected Operations
  • Private Support Handoff

Prerequisites

  • A modern supported browser or runtime
  • The current installed product and service version
  • A new demonstration workspace
  • No confidential, personal, credential, or production information
  • A named reviewer for consequential output

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Product Taxonomyassign Product, Version, Component, Issue Type, and Releaseconsistent routing contextsynthetic-demo
2Support Articlescreate detailed guides as sc_support_article draftssearchable product documentationsynthetic-demo
3Documentation Collectionsgroup six guides into a hierarchical product collectiona browsable documentation setsynthetic-demo

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.
  2. Confirm the product and backend version before creating data.
  3. Create a synthetic workspace named PRODUCT-SUPPORT-PLATFORM-RETROFIT-001.
  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Product Taxonomy

Action: assign Product, Version, Component, Issue Type, and Release.

Expected result: consistent routing context.

2. Support Articles

Action: create detailed guides as sc_support_article drafts.

Expected result: searchable product documentation.

3. Documentation Collections

Action: group six guides into a hierarchical product collection.

Expected result: a browsable documentation set.

4. Article Types

Action: classify Getting Started, How-to, Troubleshooting, and Technical Reference.

Expected result: filtered content.

5. Guided Resolution

Action: match an endpoint error to articles, issues, releases, and next steps.

Expected result: a product-aware route.

6. Known Issues

Action: publish symptom, severity, state, workaround, and resolution.

Expected result: a public issue record.

Expected output

  • consistent routing context
  • searchable product documentation
  • a browsable documentation set
  • filtered content
  • a product-aware route
  • a public issue record
  • A saved record that reopens without data loss
  • An export or handoff that identifies product, version, artifact, source, and review state

Verification

  • Every sample value remains attached to its identifier and classification.
  • Warnings remain visible and are not silently treated as approval.
  • The saved workspace reopens in a clean session.
  • The product and version shown in output match the installed release.
  • Another reviewer can understand how the output was produced.

Responsible use

Public support records must exclude identity, private cases, correspondence, documents, credentials, and confidential details.

articles.title: Product Support and Feedback Platform: Installation and Configuration

articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators

articles.content:
Product Support and Feedback Platform · How-to Guide

Public product documentation, guided resolution, known issues, releases, ideas, voting, surveys, feedback, gaps, onboarding, import, governance, repository sync, reliability, incidents, journeys, operations, and private handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 5.1.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Product Support and Feedback Platform, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Product Taxonomyassign Product, Version, Component, Issue Type, and Releaseconsistent routing contextsynthetic-demo
2Support Articlescreate detailed guides as sc_support_article draftssearchable product documentationsynthetic-demo
3Documentation Collectionsgroup six guides into a hierarchical product collectiona browsable documentation setsynthetic-demo
4Article Typesclassify Getting Started, How-to, Troubleshooting, and Technical Referencefiltered contentsynthetic-demo
5Guided Resolutionmatch an endpoint error to articles, issues, releases, and next stepsa product-aware routesynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 22 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Public support records must exclude identity, private cases, correspondence, documents, credentials, and confidential details.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.

  19. 19. Platform Incidents: coordinate a cross-product public incident. Confirm an incident record and affected products.
  20. 20. Cross-Product Journeys: guide users from data to analysis to decision. Confirm a connected support journey.
  21. 21. Connected Operations: review dossiers, blockers, readiness, actions, and snapshots. Confirm an operations report.
  22. 22. Private Support Handoff: send consented product/version/problem context to Contact and Engagement. Confirm a short-lived private handoff.

Save and review

  1. Save the final workspace under the sample workspace ID.
  2. Close and reopen it in a clean session.
  3. Compare every feature result with the sample expected_result field.
  4. Document unresolved differences and do not hide warnings.
  5. Export one complete artifact bundle.
  6. Send one supported handoff to an integration and verify its receipt.

Supported integrations

  • Contact and Engagement Platform
  • All Sustainable Catalyst products
  • GitHub
  • Platform Core

Expected output

  • consistent routing context
  • searchable product documentation
  • a browsable documentation set
  • filtered content
  • a product-aware route
  • a public issue record
  • a release record with related guidance
  • a public idea and official status
  • a bounded support signal
  • private responses and reviewed analysis
  • a quality signal
  • a documentation-gap signal
  • a ranked editorial opportunity
  • a support profile
  • an integrity-tracked batch
  • a human publication trail
  • a synchronization finding
  • an advisory reliability dossier
  • an incident record and affected products
  • a connected support journey
  • an operations report
  • a short-lived private handoff
  • A complete saved workspace
  • A portable export
  • A validated cross-product handoff receipt

Verification

  • Every CSV feature row has a corresponding saved result.
  • The JSON workspace ID and product version are preserved.
  • The export can be inspected without temporary browser state.
  • The receiving product identifies source, destination, schema, artifact, and review state.
  • A human reviewer can reproduce or audit the demonstration.

Responsible use

Public support records must exclude identity, private cases, correspondence, documents, credentials, and confidential details.

Replace synthetic data only with information you are authorized to use, after reassessing licensing, privacy, uncertainty, and review obligations.

articles.title: Product Support and Feedback Platform: Troubleshooting and Recovery
articles.summary: Diagnose and recover common Product Support and Feedback Platform failures without losing state.
articles.audience: Product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators
articles.prerequisites: Current product release; synthetic sample; no private data; human review
articles.collection: Product Support and Feedback Platform User Guides
articles.content:
Product Support and Feedback Platform · Troubleshooting

Public product documentation, guided resolution, known issues, releases, ideas, voting, surveys, feedback, gaps, onboarding, import, governance, repository sync, reliability, incidents, journeys, operations, and private handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Last verified version: 5.1.0

Reading time: 18–30 minutes

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide uses an evidence-first diagnostic sequence for Product Support and Feedback Platform. Preserve state before changing configuration. Determine whether the failure comes from input data, permissions, version identity, browser assets, backend health, schema compatibility, external dependencies, or a product defect.

Prerequisites

  • Exact error message and time
  • Product, backend, package, and schema versions
  • Affected route, feature, record ID, and user role
  • A safe backup or export
  • The synthetic sample for a reference test

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Product Taxonomyassign Product, Version, Component, Issue Type, and Releaseconsistent routing contextsynthetic-demo
2Support Articlescreate detailed guides as sc_support_article draftssearchable product documentationsynthetic-demo
3Documentation Collectionsgroup six guides into a hierarchical product collectiona browsable documentation setsynthetic-demo
4Article Typesclassify Getting Started, How-to, Troubleshooting, and Technical Referencefiltered contentsynthetic-demo
5Guided Resolutionmatch an endpoint error to articles, issues, releases, and next stepsa product-aware routesynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 22 feature rows.

Procedure

  1. Stop repeated imports, submissions, migrations, or destructive retries.
  2. Record URL, feature, action, role, versions, browser/runtime, and expected result.
  3. Run the same feature row in a new synthetic workspace.
  4. If the sample fails, inspect health, logs, browser console, network response, and validation details.
  5. If the sample succeeds, compare real input schema, units, permissions, size, source, and status with the sample.
  6. Check version and schema identity before clearing caches or reinstalling.
  7. Apply the smallest reversible change.
  8. Repeat the sample and real workflow, then document the result.

Common problems

SymptomLikely causeSafe response
Articles not visiblerecords are draft or filters/taxonomies exclude themreview, publish, and verify context
Import duplicatesstable keys or fingerprints are inconsistentuse content keys and review the batch
Guided resolution weakpublished coverage or terminology is insufficientpublish relevant articles and synonyms
Private data riskcase detail entered a public recordremove it and use private handoff

Escalation package

  • Product and version
  • Feature and component
  • Exact error text
  • Reproduction steps using synthetic data
  • Expected and actual result
  • Redacted log excerpt without secrets or personal data
  • Whether a clean sample reproduces the problem

Expected output

  • A reproducible diagnosis or bounded unresolved issue
  • Preserved user and system state
  • A documented workaround when available
  • A support-ready issue summary without private data

Verification

  • The synthetic feature succeeds after the fix.
  • The original workflow succeeds or remains safely isolated.
  • Version and health identity are consistent.
  • No duplicates, stale locks, or unintended cache artifacts remain.
  • The resolution is reflected in a support article, known issue, or release record when appropriate.

Responsible use

Public support records must exclude identity, private cases, correspondence, documents, credentials, and confidential details.

Never place credentials, API keys, private URLs, personal records, client documents, or confidential logs in a public article or issue.

Product Support and Feedback Platform · Technical Reference

Public product documentation, guided resolution, known issues, releases, ideas, voting, surveys, feedback, gaps, onboarding, import, governance, repository sync, reliability, incidents, journeys, operations, and private handoffs.

Audience: product users, administrators, reviewers, and integrators.

Purpose

This reference summarizes the functional contract, record expectations, lifecycle, validation, export, compatibility, integration, privacy, and release-review requirements for Product Support and Feedback Platform.

Prerequisites

  • Current release notes and repository documentation
  • A test environment
  • A backup and rollback plan
  • Access to product health or package checks
  • The synthetic all-feature sample

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

3Documentation Collectionsgroup six guides into a hierarchical product collectiona browsable documentation setsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 22 feature rows.

Technical contract

Canonical identity

FieldValue
Product slugproduct-support-platform
Product nameProduct Support and Feedback Platform
Verified version5.1.0
Content pack1.0.0
Default article statedraft
Human reviewrequired

Functional surface

CapabilitySample operationExpected artifact
Product Taxonomyassign Product, Version, Component, Issue Type, and Releaseconsistent routing context
Support Articlescreate detailed guides as sc_support_article draftssearchable product documentation
Documentation Collectionsgroup six guides into a hierarchical product collectiona browsable documentation set
Article Typesclassify Getting Started, How-to, Troubleshooting, and Technical Referencefiltered content
Guided Resolutionmatch an endpoint error to articles, issues, releases, and next stepsa product-aware route

Release Intelligenceconnect current, planned, maintenance, superseded, and retired releasesa release record with related guidance
Public Ideaspublish a moderated feature proposala public idea and official status
Votingrecord advisory support without automatic prioritizationa bounded support signal

Cross-Product Journeysguide users from data to analysis to decisiona connected support journey

Private Support Handoffsend consented product/version/problem context to Contact and Engagementa short-lived private handoff

Core record fields

  • Stable record or artifact ID
  • Product and schema version
  • Created and updated time
  • Owner or responsible role
  • Lifecycle or review state
  • Source and provenance
  • Units, period, geography, classification, or audience where applicable
  • Validation findings and uncertainty
  • Privacy and publication boundary
  • Export or handoff integrity metadata

Lifecycle

Use a human-controlled lifecycle such as draft → review → approved/published, with revise, superseded, archived, withdrawn, failed, and recovered states where appropriate. Automated analysis may recommend; it must not silently approve, publish, or create private cases.

Validation

  • Required-field and type checks
  • Identifier and relationship checks
  • Unit, period, geography, method, and classification compatibility
  • Product and schema compatibility
  • Source, evidence, and provenance completeness
  • Permission, privacy, and publication-boundary checks
  • Deterministic checksum or fingerprint validation for portable artifacts

Integrations

  • Contact and Engagement Platform
  • All Sustainable Catalyst products
  • GitHub
  • Platform Core

Send the minimum required fields through a typed contract. Preserve source product, destination, schema, artifact ID, review state, provenance, consent or privacy boundary, and receipt. Private Contact and Engagement records never belong in public support or research artifacts.

Release review

  1. Confirm interface, service, manifest, package, and schema versions.
  2. Validate the all-feature sample.
  3. Run product tests and integrity checks.
  4. Test one export and each supported handoff category.
  5. Document breaking changes and migration path.
  6. Update Last Verified Version before publishing this article.

Expected output

  • A complete feature contract
  • A validated synthetic export
  • A compatibility decision
  • A documented integration receipt
  • A release-integrity report

Verification

  • Product slug, version, schema, and IDs agree.
  • Every documented feature maps to an actual interface, command, route, or record.
  • The sample files produce the stated artifacts.
  • Integrations preserve provenance and privacy.
  • Migration, rollback, support, and release documentation are current.

Responsible use

Public support records must exclude identity, private cases, correspondence, documents, credentials, and confidential details.

Prerequisites

Sample data

Procedure

  1. Open the product from its canonical Sustainable Catalyst page or administrative route.

  3. Create a synthetic workspace named CONTACT-ENGAGEMENT-RETROFIT-001.
  4. Download the CSV and JSON examples supplied below.
  5. Use the feature matrix to complete the first six feature checkpoints.
  6. Save, close, and reopen the workspace to verify persistence.
  7. Inspect warnings, sources, units, permissions, and lifecycle state.
  8. Export or hand off only after completing the verification checklist.

1. Compact Intake

Action: submit essential advisory context with name, email, purpose, and summary.

Expected result: a private inquiry record.

2. Advanced Intake

Action: add timeline, budget range, product, goals, and institutional context.

Expected result: a routed private request.

3. Conditional Routing

Action: show fields and workflow for support, advisory, media, or collaboration.

Expected result: a purpose-specific intake path.

4. Inquiry Records

Expected result: an auditable private record.

5. Support Cases

Expected result: a case with requester and product context.

6. Secure Document Upload

Action: upload an authorized redacted project brief outside the public Media Library.

Expected result: a controlled file record.

Expected output

  • a private inquiry record
  • a routed private request
  • a purpose-specific intake path
  • an auditable private record
  • a case with requester and product context
  • a controlled file record
  • A saved record that reopens without data loss
  • An export or handoff that identifies product, version, artifact, source, and review state

Verification

  • Every sample value remains attached to its identifier and classification.
  • Warnings remain visible and are not silently treated as approval.
  • The saved workspace reopens in a clean session.
  • The product and version shown in output match the installed release.
  • Another reviewer can understand how the output was produced.

Responsible use

Apply least privilege, secure uploads, consent, retention limits, incident response, and strict separation from public support content.

Contact and Engagement Platform · How-to Guide

Status: Draft for product and editorial review.

Contents
  • Purpose
  • Prerequisites
  • Sample data
  • Procedure
  • Expected output
  • Verification
  • Responsible use

Purpose

This guide covers a reversible installation or update of Contact and Engagement Platform, including baseline capture, package identity, dependencies, routes, permissions, service health, sample validation, rollback, and documentation handoff.

Prerequisites

  • A verified backup
  • Administrative or repository access
  • The canonical release archive and checksum
  • A documented rollback path
  • A test workspace and the synthetic sample below

Sample data

This article uses a fictional Resilient Community Retrofit Program. The files contain one demonstration row for every documented feature, so each capability can be tested without private or production data.

Download CSV feature examples · Download JSON workspace example

Preview

#FeatureSample actionExpected resultClassification
1Compact Intakesubmit essential advisory context with name, email, purpose, and summarya private inquiry recordsynthetic-demo
2Advanced Intakeadd timeline, budget range, product, goals, and institutional contexta routed private requestsynthetic-demo
3Conditional Routingshow fields and workflow for support, advisory, media, or collaborationa purpose-specific intake pathsynthetic-demo
4Inquiry Recordspreserve original submission, consent, source, and lifecyclean auditable private recordsynthetic-demo
5Support Casescreate a private case from a consented support handoffa case with requester and product contextsynthetic-demo

The downloadable files include all 22 feature rows.

Procedure

1. Capture the baseline

  1. Record the installed interface, backend, package, and schema versions.
  2. Export or back up product-owned state.
  3. Record canonical routes, shortcodes, service URLs, repository branch, and environment variables.
  4. Check for incomplete migrations or uncommitted changes.

2. Install or update

  1. Verify the archive root, manifest, checksum, and version identity.
  2. Install through the product-supported WordPress, package, or service path.
  3. Run syntax, structure, contract, and product tests before replacing the working release.
  4. Apply database or schema migrations only after the backup is confirmed.
  5. Start or refresh the backend and verify its health response.

3. Configure

  1. Set documented endpoints and secret references; never store secret values in public code.
  2. Assign least-privilege roles and product ownership.
  3. Confirm product, version, component, issue, release, collection, and article-type context where supported.
  4. Clear only the caches affected by changed assets or routes.

4. Validate

  1. Import the feature-example CSV or JSON.
  2. Test every feature row in a nonproduction workspace.
  3. Compare actual results with the expected_result field.
  4. Reopen state after a clean reload.
  5. Record differences as draft support issues before enabling real use.

Expected output

  • One consistent product version across interface, service, manifest, package, and export
  • Healthy routes and dependencies
  • A complete synthetic feature test
  • A verified backup and rollback point
  • No credentials or private data in public artifacts

Verification

  • PHP, Python, R, JavaScript, or package checks pass where applicable.
  • The product loads in an incognito or clean session.
  • The sample data can be created, saved, reopened, exported, and removed.
  • Permissions prevent unauthorized administrative or private access.
  • The previous release can be restored.

Responsible use

Apply least privilege, secure uploads, consent, retention limits, incident response, and strict separation from public support content.

Do not delete backups or the prior working release until the installed version and all sample features have been validated.
